paper_title: Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Open-world embodied agents must solve long-horizon tasks where the main bottleneck is not single-step planning quality but how interaction experience is organized and evolved. To this end, we present Steve-Evolving, a non-parametric self-evolving framework that tightly couples fine-grained execution diagnosis with dual-track knowledge distillation in a closed loop. The method follows three phases: Experience Anchoring, Experience Distillation, and Knowledge-Driven Closed-Loop Control. In detail, Experience Anchoring solidifies each subgoal attempt into a structured experience tuple with a fixed schema (pre-state, action, diagnosis-result, and post-state) and organizes it in a three-tier experience space with multi-dimensional indices (e.g., condition signatures, spatial hashing, and semantic tags) plus rolling summarization for efficient and auditable recall. To ensure sufficient information density for attribution, the execution layer provides compositional diagnosis signals beyond binary outcomes, including state-difference summaries, enumerated failure causes, continuous indicators, and stagnation/loop detection. Moreover, successful trajectories of Experience Distillation are generalized into reusable skills with explicit preconditions and verification criteria, while failures are distilled into executable guardrails that capture root causes and forbid risky operations at both subgoal and task granularities. Besides, Knowledge-Driven Closed-Loop Control retrieved skills and guardrails are injected into an LLM planner, and diagnosis-triggered local replanning updates the active constraints online, forming a continual evolution process without any model parameter updates. Experiments on the long-horizon suite of Minecraft MCU demonstrate consistent improvements over static-retrieval baselines.

sections.content: 2026-03-16 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Zhengwei Xie1,, Zhisheng Chen2,, Ziyan Weng3,, Tingyu Wu2, Chenglong Li4, Vireo Zhang, Kun Wang5 1University of Science and Technology of China 2University of the Chinese Academy of Sciences 3City University of Hong Kong (Dongguan) 4Tsinghua University 5Nanyang Technological University # xiezhengwei@mail.ustc.edu.cn, chenzhisheng25@mails.ucas.ac.cn, 72510283@cityu-dg.edu.cn Equal Contribution https://github.com/xzw-ustc/Steve-Evolving Corresponding Author. Abstract Open-world embodied agents must solve long-horizon tasks where the main bottleneck is not single-step planning quality but how interaction experience is organized and evolved. To this end, we present Steve-Evolving, non-parametric self-evolving framework that tightly couples fine-grained execution diagnosis with dual-track knowledge distillation in closed loop. The method follows three phases: ❶Experience Anchoring, ❷Experience Distillation, and ❸Knowledge-Driven Closed-Loop Control. In detail, Experience Anchoring solidifies each subgoal attempt into structured experience tuple with fixed schema (pre-state, action, diagnosis-result, and post-state) and organizes it in three-tier experience space with multi-dimensional indices (e.g., condition signatures, spatial hashing, and semantic tags) plus rolling summarization for efficient and auditable recall. To ensure sufficient information density for attribution, the execution layer provides compositional diagnosis signals beyond binary outcomes, including state-difference summaries, enumerated failure causes, continuous indicators, and stagnation/loop detection. Moreover, successful trajectories of Experience Distillation are generalized into reusable skills with explicit preconditions and verification criteria, while failures are distilled into executable guardrails that capture root causes and forbid risky operations at both subgoal and task granularities. Besides, Knowledge-Driven Closed-Loop Control retrieved skills and guardrails are injected into an LLM planner, and diagnosis-triggered local replanning updates the active constraints online, forming continual evolution process without any model parameter updates. Experiments on the long-horizon suite of Minecraft MCU demonstrate consistent improvements over static-retrieval baselines, larger gains on high-dependency task groups, and increasing success rates as experience accumulates. 1. Introduction Building an embodied agent capable of autonomously accomplishing long-horizon compound tasks in open worlds has long been core vision in the field of artificial intelligence [Gupta et al., 2021][Luo et al., 2025]. The rapid advances of large language models (LLMs) and multimodal large models in instruction understanding and logical reasoning have opened up new possibilities for realizing In open-world sandbox this vision [Achiam et al., 2023, Brown et al., 2020, Liu et al., 2023]. environments represented by Minecraft [Baker et al., 2022, Fan et al., 2022, Johnson et al., 2016, Lifshitz et al., 2023], series of agent systems have emerged that can decompose complex goals into subtask sequences and execute them interactively through low-level controllers [Li et al., 2025, Wang et al., 2023, 2024]. However, when the task complexity escalates from single-step operations to long-horizon scenarios requiring the sequential completion of multiple interdependent subgoals [Sutton et al., 1999], there remains significant performance gap between these systems and human players. Notably, the root of this gap does not lie in the quality of single-step decision-making, as existing large models can already generate reasonable plans for many independent subtasks [Huang et al., 2022]. The real factor limiting the upper bound of capability is more fundamental issue: as 6 2 0 2 3 1 ] . [ 1 1 3 1 3 1 . 3 0 6 2 : r Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Figure 1 Diagnosis-triggered knowledge-driven planning (horizontal layout). failed subgoal execution is first analyzed by fine-grained diagnostic signals (e.g., loop detection and unsafe-area failures), then distilled into two reusable knowledge types: guardrails that forbid risky actions under specified triggers, and skills that summarize successful procedures with explicit preconditions and verification. The retrieved guardrails and skills are injected into the LLM planner to guide safer planning and to trigger local replanning when failures recur, forming closed-loop of diagnosis, distillation, and knowledge-driven control. the task horizon extends, the agent accumulates continuous experience through sustained interaction, and the way it organizes and evolves such experience directly determines whether it can continuously benefit from past successes and failures[Deng et al., 2023, Wang et al., 2026]. This issue is fundamentally important because the organization of experience largely determines the level of information the agent can extract from historical interactions, thereby constraining the upper bound of its capability growth. An intuitive observation illuminates the core of this problem: the growth of human professional competence is never simple accumulation of past experiences[Anderson, 1982]. An experienced miner is more efficient and safe than novice not because he remembers more details of individual operations, but because he gradually generalizes these experiences into operating procedures and risk prediction criteria through repeated practice, such as "must check ventilation before going down the mine" and "evacuate immediately when abnormal sounds are detected". In this process, raw experiences undergo continuous evolution from specific events to behavioral patterns, and from behavioral patterns to transferable rules. It can be said that what underpins human continuous improvement in complex environments is the ability of hierarchical experience evolution, rather than the growth of experience volume. This observation provides direct insights for the design of agent systems: if an agents experience remains in its original form at the time of recording, no matter how many historical cases it accumulates, it is essentially performing retrieval in an ever-expanding instance library rather than making decisions based on continuously refined knowledge system. review of current agent methods reveals diverse explorations in experience management strategies. JARVIS-1 [Wang et al., 2024] adds complete trajectories as key-value pairs to multimodal memory 2 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Figure 2 Minecraft technology progression from wooden to diamond tiers, illustrating the hierarchy of tools, materials, and equipment used to construct long-horizon tasks for embodied agent evaluation. bank after each successful execution, and retrieves the most similar historical cases via CLIP vector similarity [Radford et al., 2021] for LLMs to reference in subsequent tasks; its memory bank grows continuously with task progression, exhibiting characteristics of lifelong learning [Parisi et al., 2019]. Optimus-1 [Li et al., 2025] further divides memory into two dimensions: knowledge and experience. It discovers synthetic relationships between items through free exploration and extracts them into Directed Heterogeneous Knowledge Graph (HDKG), while storing summarized multimodal information from the execution process in an Abstract Multimodal Experience Pool (AMEP), continuously enriching both during iterative learning. In particular, the graph-structured representation of synthetic relationships in HDKG already reflects the awareness of extracting structured knowledge from raw interactions. In recent years, series of self-evolution methods have emerged in the field of general LLM agents focusing on how to extract behavioral knowledge from interactive experience. Reflexion[Shinn et al., 2023] extracts lessons learned from failures through verbal self-reflection, ExpeL[Zhao et al., 2024] generalizes transferable behavioral insights from successful and failed trajectories, and Voyager [Wang et al., 2023] persistently stores verified skills as reusable skill libraries in code form [Liang et al., 2023]. These works demonstrate that the basic idea of generalizing behavioral knowledge from experience to feed back into subsequent decision-making has been extensively explored [Madaan et al., 2023, Yao et al., 2022]. However, when focusing on open-world embodied agents, we find that the above methods face structural adaptation challenge: failure modes in embodied environments are far more complex than those in pure text or code tasks, as they are often intertwined with multi-dimensional factors such as spatial navigation, physical interaction, GUI operation, and resource status. Accurate attribution of such failures requires fine-grained structured diagnostic signals from the execution layer, rather than relying solely on verbal post-hoc reflection on execution trajectories. In current open-world embodied agents, the connection between this diagnosis and generalization has not been systematically established. JARVIS-1 [Wang et al., 2024] only retains the original form of successful trajectories and discards failed experiences; although Optimus-1 [Li et al., 2025] preserves both successful and failed Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation cases, it only uses them as reference examples for in-context learning for LLMs, without leveraging execution-layer diagnostic signals to attribute failures to actionable error-avoidance constraints. More generally, existing reflection-based methods can generate natural language summaries of failures, but their attribution accuracy and operability are significantly constrained in the absence of structured diagnostic inputsthey can tell the planner that "the last attempt failed", but struggle to precisely identify "which specific embodied state anomaly caused the failure and what computable execution constraints should be imposed to avoid repeating the mistake". To address the above issues, this paper proposes Steve-Evolving, hierarchical experience evolution paradigm for open-world embodied agents, whose core lies in the tight coupling of fine-grained embodied diagnostic signals and structured knowledge distillation. This enables experience to not only be recorded and retrieved, but also gradually refined into accurate behavioral knowledge that can directly constrain planning and execution through sustained interaction. Toward this goal, we construct continuous experience evolution pipeline. On this pipeline, the execution result of each subgoal is first recorded as structured document with fixed schema, including state snapshots before and after execution, success/failure judgment, and detailed diagnostic information, with efficient retrieval enabled via multi-dimensional indexing. As interaction progresses, these instancelevel records at the document layer are further generalized into two types of higher-level knowledge representations: successful experiences are refined into reusable skill descriptions covering effective steps, preconditions, and inspection criteria; failed experiences are refined into defensive avoidance rules covering failure symptoms, root causes, and operations to be avoided. The generalized skills and avoidance rules are stored in cross-task shared knowledge base, which is retrieved and injected into the LLMs context [Brown et al., 2020, Lewis et al., 2020] during the planning phase of subsequent tasks. This allows the planner to reference past effective practices and avoid known failure modes when generating new action plans. Experience generated from the execution of new tasks then enters the same evolution pipeline, thus forming closed loop of continuous knowledge accumulation and feedback. On this pipeline, experience evolves step by step from raw interaction records to structured behavioral knowledge, and then from behavioral knowledge to explicit constraints on planning, with each evolutionary step enhancing the reusability of information. key prerequisite for the effective operation of the above paradigm is that the raw experience itself must have sufficient information density. If the executor only returns binary judgment of success or failure, subsequent distillation lacks signal basis for distinguishing different failure modes and generating targeted guardrails. To this end, this paper also constructs set of fine-grained execution diagnosis systems, including compositional execution monitoring with 13 types of check specifications, structured attribution with 11 enumerated failure causes, and loop and stagnation detector that adaptively switches detection strategies according to the semantic type of subgoals. These mechanisms endow each interaction with diagnostic granularity far exceeding binary success/failure, serving as the signal foundation for the accurate evolution of experience. Experiments in the Minecraft [Johnson et al., 2016] open-world environment show that the proposed experience evolution paradigm significantly outperforms baseline methods using static experience retrieval on long-horizon task benchmarks. Ablation studies verify the indispensability of each link in the experience life cycle for the final performance. In addition, the agents task success rate exhibits increasing trend with accumulated experience, directly confirming the driving effect of hierarchical experience evolution on continuous capability improvementa trend not observed in baseline methods relying solely on instance accumulation. The main contributions of this paper are summarized as follows: We propose hierarchical experience evolution paradigm for open-world embodied agents, redefining interactive experience from static retrieval corpus to structured assets with life cycle. 4 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Figure 3 Overview of the proposed experience evolution framework for open-world embodied agents. Embodied interactions are first diagnosed through fine-grained execution signals and recorded as structured experience documents. dual-track distillation mechanism then extracts reusable skills from successful trajectories and guardrails from failure cases. The distilled knowledge is injected into the LLM planner to guide decision-making and trigger replanning when failures accumulate, forming closed-loop process of experience accumulation and knowledge-driven control. Experience undergoes step-by-step evolution from raw signals to structured documents, from document instances to abstract knowledge, and from knowledge to planning constraints through sustained interaction, providing new technical route for non-parametric self-evolving agents. We design an experience space based on structured documents and its supporting three-tier compositional recall mechanism, achieving high-fidelity, auditable, and hierarchical retrievalsupported experience management. We propose dual-track experience distillation mechanism, establishing an automatic extraction closed loop from fine-grained execution diagnosis to defensive planning constraints in open-world embodied agents, and supporting continuous accumulation and transfer of distilled knowledge through two-stage cross-task routing. We verify the effectiveness of the paradigm on Minecraft long-horizon task benchmarks, and empirically demonstrate the significant advantage of hierarchical experience evolution over instance accumulation strategies in continuous capability improvement. 2. Method 2.1. Setup and Notation The method proposed in this paper aims to build self-evolving embodied agent in open-world sandbox environments such as Minecraft. The continuous evolution of the agent should not rely on storing massive raw video frames or action trajectories, but on gradually distilling complex embodied interactive experience into structured professional knowledge [Packer et al., 2023, Park et al., 2023]. This distillation process requires three basic conditions: first, high-freedom interaction data in open worlds must be recorded with high fidelity and structure, enabling each mining or placement behavior to be accurately retrieved and accessed at the field level; second, an abstraction mechanism is needed 5 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation to generalize reusable embodied skills and defensive constraints from massive interactions; finally, the distilled knowledge must be able to be reinjected into the planning and execution of subsequent tasks to form closed loop. Corresponding to these three conditions, our method unfolds into three closely connected phases: Experience Anchoring solidifies embodied interaction signals into structured documents and establishes multi-dimensional spatial indexing (Section 2.2), Experience Distillation extracts transferable skills and environmental negative constraints from the documents (Section 2.3), and Knowledge-Driven Closed-Loop Control retrieves and injects the distilled products into the large language model (LLM) planner, triggering dynamic replanning when physical failures such as terrain blocking occur (Section 2.4). These three phases cycle as the agent continuously explores the Minecraft world, enabling it to accumulate survival and construction capabilities task by task without updating the model parameters 𝜃. 2.2. Experience Anchoring: From Embodied Interaction to Structured Documents In 3D voxel world with extremely high state complexity such as Minecraft, the premise of experience accumulation is that raw trajectories can provide sufficiently clear low-level physical details. If the low-level executor only returns binary signal of "task success" or "task failure", the system lacks the basis to distinguish different failure modes such as "terrain occlusion", "lack of specific tools", or "container GUI blockage". Therefore, this method starts from the low-level controller and introduces structured diagnosis mechanism to solidify the 3D environment feedback during execution into multi-dimensional state features. Formally, we define the state space of the Markov Decision Process (MDP) [Sutton et al., 1998] for the Minecraft environment as (including player coordinates, health points, visual images, inventory, etc.) and the action space of subgoals as A. For subgoal 𝑎𝑡 generated in the planning phase (e.g., craft wooden_pickaxe), the system binds it with set of prior rule-based environment check functions 𝜙𝑎𝑡 : {0, 1}, covering 13 types of state observations such as inventory quantity comparison, equipment holding status, 3D coordinate proximity detection, and furnace/crafting table progress. Knowledge-Driven Closed-Loop Exploration Control The long-term survival of the agent in the open world ultimately depends on how the extracted environmental rules effectively intervene in the long-term planning path of the large language model 𝜋𝜃. The planner autoregressively decodes the optimal workflow distribution 𝑃: 𝑃(𝑎1:𝑁 𝑠0, 𝑔, 𝑀0, K) = 𝑁 (cid:214) 𝑖=1 (cid:16) 𝑎𝑖 𝜋𝜃 (cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12) 𝑎<𝑖, 𝑠0, 𝑔, 𝑀0, K𝑠𝑘𝑖𝑙𝑙, K𝑔𝑢𝑎𝑟𝑑 (cid:17) (11) In practical operation, the positive blueprints K𝑠𝑘𝑖𝑙𝑙 act as few-shot demonstrations to ensure action coherence, while safety constraints K𝑔𝑢𝑎𝑟𝑑 serve as negative prompt guardrails [Ahn et al., 2022], preventing the generation of high-risk subgoals. During subsequent continuous execution, if unexpected terrain occlusion or hostile mob encounters cause the cumulative execution error to exceed the safety threshold ((cid:205)(1 𝑜𝑡) 𝐾𝑡𝑜𝑙), the system triggers the Local Replanning Mechanism. The agent decisively halts the current route, formulates 𝑛𝑒𝑤, appends it to its active prompt context, and resamples new localized blockage constraint 𝑘 corrected detour strategy: 𝑃 𝑡+1 𝑁 (cid:214) 𝑗=𝑡+1 (cid:16) 𝑎 𝑗 𝜋𝜃 (cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12) 𝑠𝑡, 𝑔𝑟𝑒𝑚, 𝑀 𝑡 , K𝑔𝑢𝑎𝑟𝑑 {𝑘 𝑛𝑒𝑤} (cid:17) (12) This real-time diagnosis and dynamic replanning mechanism enables the agent to survive autonomously without human intervention. The infinite closed-loop framework of embodied monitoring (E) abstract distillation (K) knowledge intervention (𝜋𝜃) low-level execution (D) rigorously empowers the agent to enhance its capability ceiling as its interaction lifetime expands. 8 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Table 1 MCU tech-tree evaluation suite with 7 task groups. Group Task Num Example Task Tools/weapons; wood/planks/saplings gathering Tools/weapons; stone/cobblestone mining Tools/weapons; iron ore mining; villager trading Tools; golden foods; repairing golden gear Components; basic circuits; redstone clock Tools; diamond mining; repairing/trading Leather/iron armor; washing; helmet repair Wooden Stone Iron Golden Redstone Diamond Armor 11 10 16 7 6 7 3. Experiments 3.1. Experimental Setup 3.1.1. Environment Minecraft sandbox. We evaluate all methods in the MineRLHumanSurvival-v0 [Guss et al., 2019], built on Project Malmo (v0.37.0) [Johnson et al., 2016] and the MCU (minestudio) interface [Zheng et al., 2025]. The interface provides human-like controls (continuous camera control with discrete movement/interaction keys), matching open-world embodied constraints [Liu et al., 2025]. Randomness. All episodes run in Survival mode. We reset environments via forceReset with default terrain generation and evaluate each method over multiple independent seeds. Simulation. We set MsPerTick=50 ms (20 TPS). Agents receive egocentric observations at 640360, and we record videos at 20 FPS. Start state. Each episode starts with an empty inventory (no pre-loaded AgentStart items), unless stated otherwise. 3.1.2. Tasks and Benchmarks MCU tech-tree task suite. We adopt subset of the MCU benchmark [Zheng et al., 2025] organized along the Minecraft technology tree. The suite contains 70 tasks grouped into 7 stages, covering crafting, mining, trading, repairing, eating, and building. This forms curriculum from short-horizon goals to long-horizon tasks with deeper prerequisite chains. Table 1 summarizes the 7 task groups and representative task types. Evaluation protocol. Each episode terminates upon task success or budget exhaustion. We report success rate (SR) averaged over multiple seeds. All methods share the same environment configuration, start state, and per-episode interaction budget. Fairness and instantiation. All methods use the same observation/action interface and are evaluated on the same task suite with identical environment settings (Section 3.1.1) and budgets. Among compared agents, Jarvis-1, Optimus-1 and Steve-Evolving use LLM-based planners, while STEVE1 [Lifshitz et al., 2023] does not. For controlled comparison, all LLM-based methods use the same backbone family. We experiment with five representative LLMs: qwen3.5-flash [Qwen Team, 2026], qwen3.5-plus [Qwen Team, 2026], GLM-4.7 [Zeng et al., 2025], gemini-3-pro, and gemini-3-flash. For the human baseline, we report average SR and completion time across players and repetitions; other components are kept identical unless noted. Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Table 2 Main results on the MCU task suite (Success Rate, SR, %). Task abbreviations: Wo.: Wooden, St.: Stone, Ir.: Iron, Go.: Gold, Re.: Redstone, Di.: Diamond, Ar.: Armor. Best results are highlighted in bold. Method Wo. St. Ir. Go. Re. Di. Ar. Overall Qwen3.5-flash +Jarvis-1 +Optimus-1 +Steve-Evolving Qwen3.5-plus +Jarvis-1 +Optimus-1 +Steve-Evolving GLM-4.7 +Jarvis-1 +Optimus-1 +Steve-Evolving Gemini-3-flash +Jarvis-1 +Optimus-1 +Steve-Evolving Gemini-3-pro +Jarvis-1 +Optimus-1 +Steve-Evolving Steve-1 Human-level 92.37 98.30 98.09 93.08 97.91 98. 91.79 96.63 97.36 92.99 98.70 98.63 88.14 92.75 92.61 88.73 93.60 93.48 86.87 91.40 91.18 88.58 91.76 93. 34.76 45.24 52.43 35.57 46.06 55.83 33.94 44.13 50.55 35.21 45.92 55.28 93.57 98.28 98.97 25.61 100. 89.09 94.30 94.19 18.72 100.00 35.93 46.41 57.07 2.47 100.00 10.83 8.07 10.11 11.12 7.05 13. 8.87 7.79 8.63 9.79 6.85 13.52 11.27 8.58 14.64 0.00 100.00 23.41 28.17 30.77 26.18 32.03 31. 21.82 23.06 29.08 22.96 24.48 33.73 8.39 8.21 14.58 9.03 11.42 17.06 8.08 7.95 10.92 8.92 13.06 12. 14.92 14.80 23.47 15.58 19.09 27.63 14.19 18.04 22.07 15.52 18.98 27.18 41.75 45.83 50.09 42.59 47.42 52. 40.79 45.23 48.43 42.04 46.73 52.04 23.49 25.09 35.12 0.00 100.00 9.21 14.93 15.77 0.00 100. 15.94 19.43 28.36 0.00 100.00 42.67 47.63 53.37 7.26 100.00 3.2. Main Results Table 2 reports the success rate (SR,%) on the MCU tech-tree task suite under unified evaluation protocol across five LLM backbones. Overall, Steve-Evolving achieves the best Overall SR for every backbone, indicating that the performance gains are consistent across model instantiations rather than being tied to particular planner backbone. Performance generally decreases as tasks progress along the tech tree (Wooden, Stone, Iron, Gold, Redstone, and Diamond; plus Armor), reflecting the increasing horizon length and compositional dependencies. Importantly, the advantage of Steve-Evolving is most pronounced in the later-stage groups (Iron and above), where long-horizon execution and failure recovery are critical. In contrast, early-stage groups (Wooden and Stone) are near-saturated for all methods, and therefore exhibit smaller separations. Across backbones, stronger LLMs consistently improve the absolute success rate of all LLM-planner agents, likely due to better long-horizon decomposition, stronger instruction and constraint following under explicit knowledge injection, and more robust recovery under diagnosis-triggered replanning. Importantly, our relative gains remain stable across model scales, indicating that the improvements primarily stem from the proposed closed-loop experience evolution mechanisms rather than backbone scaling alone. We also observe occasional column-level fluctuations where baseline marginally outperforms SteveEvolving for specific backbone and group; however, these localized variations do not alter the overall 10 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Figure 4 visualization of Ablation results. trend. Taken together, the results demonstrate that Steve-Evolving provides reliable improvements across backbones, with the largest benefits emerging in tasks that require sustained progress under long dependency chains. 3.3. Ablations Unless stated otherwise, we run all ablations with fixed planner backbone (Qwen3.5-plus) under the same evaluation protocol as Table 2. The reported SR values may differ slightly from Table 2 due to independent evaluation runs with different set of random seeds (Figure 4). We ablate core components of the proposed closed-loop framework and report SR on the hard task groups (Iron/Redstone/Diamond/Armor), where long-horizon dependencies are most salient. Specifically, w/o SkillDistill disables distillation of the skill library 𝐾skill, such that the agent relies on episodic evidence without converting successful trajectories into reusable skills; w/o GuardDistill disables distillation of the guardrail library 𝐾guard, removing executable constraints derived from recurring failure patterns; w/o KnowledgeVisibility removes the explicit injection of 𝐾skill and 𝐾guard into the planner context, preventing the planner from conditioning on the distilled knowledge. We additionally report Planning Only as the planner-only setting, i.e., the backbone planner without the closed-loop mechanism and without knowledge-conditioned control. The full model (Steve-Evolving) achieves SRs of 55.4/30.6/17.1/27.4 on Iron/Redstone/Diamond/Armor, respectively. Removing any component degrades performance, with the largest drop observed for w/o KnowledgeVisibility (21.9/12.6/3.1/9.5), indicating that explicit knowledge injection is crucial for realizing the benefits of the closed-loop design. Disabling guardrail distillation further reduces SR (45.4/21.7/10.1/18.3), highlighting the importance of distilled, executable constraints for mitigating repeated error patterns during long-horizon execution. In contrast, w/o SkillDistill results in smaller but consistent decrease (52.4/29.0/14.6/24.1), suggesting that skill abstraction provides complementary gains beyond episodic recall. Finally, the planner-only variant (Planning Only) attains 0.0 SR on all four groups, confirming that planning alone is insufficient for these long-horizon tasks without closed-loop knowledge grounding and recovery. 11 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation 4. Conclusion In this paper, we introduced Steve-Evolving, non-parametric self-evolving framework for open-world embodied agents that emphasizes how experience is organized and evolved rather than merely accumulated. Our paradigm treats interaction history as structured assets with life cycleprogressing from raw interaction records to auditable experience documents, then to distilled knowledge, and finally to explicit planning constraints. Central to this process is fine-grained execution diagnosis module that provides high-density signals beyond binary success/failure, enabling reliable root-cause attribution and actionable intervention. Building on these signals, Steve-Evolving performs dual-track experience distillation: the positive track generalizes reusable skills with preconditions and verifiable success criteria, while the defensive track extracts executable guardrails that forbid high-risk operations and prevent recurrent failure patterns. The distilled skills and guardrails are retrieved and injected into the LLM planner to support knowledge-driven closed-loop control, including diagnosis-triggered replanning during execution. Experiments on the Minecraft MCU long-horizon benchmark demonstrate that hierarchical experience evolution yields consistent gains over static retrieval baselines and supports sustained capability growth as experience accumulates.

sections.title: Supplementary Experimental Setup
sections.content: A. Game Introduction Minecraft (MC) is an open-ended 3D sandbox game where players interact with procedurally generated world composed of discrete blocks. From the perspective of agent research, MC naturally induces long-horizon sequential decision making: under partial observability, an agent must explore, gather resources, craft tools, and plan multi-step actions to accomplish goals. Its highly combinatorial state space and flexible objectives make Minecraft widely used testbed for studying embodied control, planning, and tool-use behaviors in complex environments. A.1. Basic Rules Minecraft world is composed of discrete blocks and populated with resources (e.g., wood, stone, ores), passive animals, and hostile mobs depending on the selected mode and difficulty. Many tasks follow characteristic progression of collecting materials, crafting intermediate items, upgrading equipment, and unlocking access to more advanced resources or regions. Figure 5 provides representative in-game screenshots that illustrate these mechanics and the typical interaction context. Figure 5 (a) Diverse block types form an explorable, harvestable, and modifiable world. (b) The crafting interface illustrates recipe-based item composition and tool progression. (c) Survival mode introduces continuous survival pressure from health, hunger, and environmental hazards. (d) The daynight cycle changes visibility and threats, making exploration and shelter building more strategic. Block interaction: Agents can mine blocks to obtain items and place blocks to construct structures or modify the terrain; tool choice affects mining speed and obtainable drops. Resource acquisition and crafting: Collected items can be combined via crafting recipes to produce tools, weapons, and utilities; the crafting graph induces clear multi-step dependencies. 15 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Survival dynamics (optional): In Survival mode, health and hunger constrain behavior; damage from falls, lava, or hostile mobs introduces risk and requires reactive control. Time and environment: Daynight cycles and biomes change visibility and threats, making navigation and shelter building strategic. A.2. Game Controls Minecraft supports both discrete and continuous control abstractions. In standard PC settings, keyboard inputs control movement (forward/back/left/right), jumping, crouching, and sprinting, while the mouse controls camera orientation and enables interactions such as mining/attacking and placing/using items. For embodied agents, these controls are commonly mapped into an action space consisting of: (i) movement primitives, (ii) camera rotation, (iii) interaction actions (break/- place/use/attack), and (iv) inventory management (hotbar selection, crafting, and item transfer). This separation of locomotion, perception (camera), and manipulation simplifies reproducible task definitions and enables fair comparisons under consistent interface. 16 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation B. Implementation Details B.1. System Overview We implement Steve-Evolving as modular closed-loop agent for Minecraft that follows plan executememorizediagnosereplan workflow. Given task specification and an initialized episode, the system first performs lightweight memory recall, then queries an LLM-based planner to generate structured plan. controller executes subgoals sequentially via routed executors under explicit completion checks, records all attempts into an append-only memory store with indexed evidence, and triggers diagnosis plus local replanning when repeated failures are observed. Modules. The system consists of (i) an LLM planner that outputs simplified PlanJSON with subgoals and observable checks, (ii) controller that orchestrates execution and failure handling, (iii) executors that ground subgoal conditions into environment actions (with optional specialized routines for crafting/smelting), (iv) an environment bridge that standardizes observation/info keys and action formatting across simulator implementations, and (v) The memory subsystem writes memory blocks, lightweight index, periodic rollups, and an LLM-driven memory agent for recall and diagnosis. In our implementation, dense embeddings are used only for lightweight candidate scoring within the indexed memory store, while final recall, diagnosis, and knowledge synthesis are performed by the memory agent over retrieved candidates rather than by standalone vector-retrieval memory bank. Data flow. At the start of an episode, the controller extracts structured state snapshot (e.g. coordinates, inventory summary, health, GUI state) and calls the memory agent to recall relevant evidence and supporting knowledge under fixed budget. The planner then generates plan as sequence of short subgoals, each specifying condition, timeout, task_kind, executor_hint, and checks. For each subgoal, the controller compiles checks into monitor function, routes execution to the appropriate executor, and logs enriched outcomes (including screenshots and info JSON) into the memory store and index. When the same subgoal fails consecutively beyond threshold 𝐾, the controller invokes diagnosis to obtain constraints and recovery actions, and performs local replanning over the remaining subgoals while preserving the original task intent. Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Table 3 Action space categories in our Minecraft environment. Primitives (examples) Semantics / Notes Category Locomotion Camera control forward / back / left / right, crouch, sprint yaw / pitch rotation (continuous or discretized) jump, Manipulation break / attack, place / use (right-click) Inventory & crafting hotbar crafting selection, (nearbyCraft/craft), item transfer Waiting/no-op no-op, hold-action templates Moves the agent through the world and supports exploration and navigation. Controls the first-person viewing direction, enabling perception and accurate targeting for downstream interactions. Supports block mining, entity attacks, block placement, and item usage; effectiveness depends on view alignment and interaction distance. Handles held-item selection, recipebased crafting, and item management required for task completion. Advances time to allow external progress (e.g., GUI updates or action completion), improving execution stability and trajectory recording. B.2. Minecraft Environment Implementation and Interface We implement Minecraft interactive environment tailored for goal-driven, long-horizon agent evaluation. The environment exposes first-person observation stream together with structured simulator signals, and supports unified action interface that covers locomotion, camera control, manipulation, and inventory/crafting operations. Each episode is initialized from task configuration that specifies both the natural-language goal and deterministic initialization commands, enabling reproducible task setups under controlled randomness. Environment configuration and dependencies. Our environment is built on top of Minecraft simulator with Gymnasium-style API (reset/step). We run episodes in Survival-style settings and reset the world at the beginning of each task. Randomness is controlled via configurable world seed; when needed, we evaluate each task over multiple seeds to report average performance and variance. The simulator runs at 20 ticks per second, and we record videos at 20 FPS for qualitative analysis. Observations. At each step, the agent receives first-person RGB frame and an accompanying info dictionary. The RGB observation is provided at compact resolution (default 128 128) for decision making, while the native render stream is maintained at 640 360 for logging and visualization. In addition, the environment provides structured state signals that are critical for evaluation and monitoring, including (i) player location (𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧), (ii) health and hunger, (iii) GUI open/closed state, (iv) inventory contents (normalized into an item-count dictionary), and (v) optional progress signals such as world time, crafting results, container contents, and furnace burn/cook progress when available. Action space. We implement an action interface that combines continuous control (camera) with discrete actions (movement and interactions). Table 3 summarizes the action categories and typical primitives used in our environment. Episode initialization and reset. Each task is specified by configuration that includes naturallanguage instruction and list of initialization commands. After reset, we regenerate the world and apply the initialization commands to construct the task scene (e.g., spawning entities or placing blocks). To ensure consistent difficulty and reproducibility, we always start from an empty inventory: 18 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation no items are preloaded at the beginning of an episode, and task-relevant resources must be obtained through interaction. To make sure the reset and scene setup are reliably reflected in subsequent observations (e.g., entity rendering and state-field updates), we advance the simulator for small number of no-op steps before the agent begins acting, reducing the impact of transient observation delays. Task success and termination. Task completion is primarily determined by explicit, observable success checks provided in the task specification (e.g., inventory thresholds or state predicates). During execution, subgoals are monitored by check functions that evaluate conditions such as inventory changes, proximity to target coordinates, GUI state, crafting outcomes, and furnace/container progress. An episode terminates when the success condition is satisfied, when fixed step/time budget is exceeded, or when the simulator indicates terminal state. All termination cases are logged with structured failure types and reasons for consistent analysis. 19 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation B.3. Prompt and Tool-Calling Details Our system uses small set of carefully designed prompts to coordinate planning, memory recall, diagnosis, and knowledge distillation. All interactions follow chat-style interface with explicit system and user roles, and the LLM is required to output strictly structured JSON or YAML objects that can be parsed and validated by downstream modules. Planner: system prompt and JSON schema. The planner is instructed to produce short, executable plans in simplified JSON schema. The system prompt specifies the required fields, allowed value ranges, and planning principles. Planner system prompt (schema card) You are Minecraft agent Planner . Please output stable , short , executable plan JSON . Use the simplified schema : { " plan_id ": " p_xxxx " , " subgoals ": [ { } " subgoal_id ": " sg_001 " , " condition ": " short English phrase , <=6 words , single action " , " timeout_s ": 60 , " task_kind ": " mine craft use combat wait " , " executor_hint ": " stevei mcu_craft wait " , " mode ": " move stay " , " checks ": [{" type ":" inv_ge " ," item ":" oak_log " ," ":1}] ] , " global _con strain ts ": [] } Planning principles : 1) Understand the main task goal first , then generate the shortest executable path ; 2) Subgoals must be semantically consistent with the main goal ; keep only necessary prerequisites ; ... 9) Output JSON only ; no explanations . 20 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Planner: user prompt with injected state and memory. The planners user prompt is constructed by concatenating the task specification, initialization commands, the initial structured state, and the retrieved memory capsule, skills, and failures. This layout exposes all necessary context to the LLM (what the task is, what the agent already has, what it previously did, and what skills/failure patterns are available), while keeping the format simple and deterministic for downstream parsing and logging. Failure Cases and Safety / Exception Handling To make failure analysis and safety behavior reproducible, we treat each subgoal attempt as structured event with rich set of observables and discrete failure taxonomy. The controller and Memory Agent reason entirely over these logged signals, rather than relying on ad-hoc heuristics hard-coded into the environment. In particular, we expose 13 state observables that capture spatial, inventory, GUI, and progress information, and we consistently label failures using 11 failure categories. Table 4 summarizes the 13 observables we record for every attempt. These signals are produced by our executor wrapper and are independent of the underlying simulator implementation. They serve three main purposes: (i) monitors built from subgoal checks evaluate completion based on these fields, (ii) loop/stall detectors operate on aggregates of these values over time, and (iii) the Memory Agent uses them to construct compact textual summaries for recall and diagnosis. Failure taxonomy. On top of these observables, we define compact but expressive taxonomy of 11 failure reasons, shown in Table 5. These reasons cover both spatial patterns (e.g., getting stuck or oscillating), interaction-level issues (e.g., GUI blocking, missing tools), and environment-level signals (e.g., early termination). Each executor call reports coarse fail_type together with one of these fine-grained reasons; we store them in the memory index so that diagnosis and skill/failure distillation can condition on structured failure information rather than raw text alone. From detection to safety-aware behavior. During execution, the controller first uses monitors built from subgoal checks to decide local success or failure; then, it aggregates the observables and failure reasons across sliding window of recent attempts to detect loops or stalls. Once repeated failures or high-confidence anomaly are observed, the controller switches from naive retrying to safety-aware behavior: it invokes the Memory Agents diagnosis prompt, obtains constraints and recovery actions, and triggers local replanning. For clearly unrecoverable situations (e.g., targets not craftable in the current action space), the controller terminates the task early and commits structured failure record, which is later distilled into guardrail rules that proactively prevent the same unsafe or unproductive behavior in future episodes. 25 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Table 5 Failure taxonomy: 11 failure categories. Supplementary Experiment We conduct curriculum-based knowledge construction experiment on the most challenging Diamond tasks. All settings share the same environment; the only difference lies in how the knowledge base is initialized and updated. The training budget is controlled by the total number of episodes. During training, we periodically evaluate on Diamond tasks. At each checkpoint, success rate is computed from repeated evaluations under multiple world seeds and then averaged across the 7 Diamond tasks. We compare the following four strategies for constructing and using the knowledge base: Cold Start (Empty KB):Throughout both training and evaluation, 𝐾skill and 𝐾guard are kept empty. The agent relies only on the base system prompt, and no cross-episode knowledge is accumulated. Diamond-only Self-learning: The agent learns exclusively on Diamond tasks; the knowledge base is built purely from Diamond trajectories, and experiences from lower tiers are never written or recalled. Curriculum PretrainFreeze: We first run long self-learning on lower-tier tasks (WoodenRedstone) to construct stable skill and failure knowledge base, then freeze this knowledge when moving to Diamond. During Diamond training and evaluation, the frozen lower-tier knowledge is reused, and new Diamond memories can be added for analysis but do not overwrite the pretrained KB. Mixed Sampling (1:1): During training, we sample Diamond and lower-tier episodes with 1:1 ratio and update unified 𝐾skill/𝐾guard online. This tests whether continuously mixing in lower-tier experience helps improve Diamond performance, and how much distribution noise this introduces. For each strategy, we train for the same number of episodes and evaluate Diamond success rates at five checkpoints, corresponding to 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100% of the total training budget. Table 6 reports mean success rates obtained from repeated evaluations under multiple world seeds and averaged across the 7 Diamond tasks. From Table 6, we observe clear and stable ranking among the four curriculum strategies throughout training. Mixed Sampling (1:1) consistently achieves the best performance at every checkpoint, improving from 6.3% at 20% of training to 18.1% after convergence. Curriculum PretrainFreeze follows closely, rising from 5.6% to 16.7%. Diamond-only Self-learning performs worse than both cross-tier strategies, but still shows substantial gains over the course of training, increasing from 26 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation 2.8% to 14.0%. In contrast, Cold Start (Empty KB) remains near the floor across all checkpoints, fluctuating only slightly between 2.5% and 3.1%, and ending at 2.8%. The early-stage advantage of transferring lower-tier knowledge is already evident at 20% training progress. Mixed Sampling exceeds Cold Start by 3.8 percentage points and Diamond-only Selflearning by 3.5 points, while Curriculum PretrainFreeze also outperforms both baselines by clear margin. This suggests that knowledge distilled from easier tasks provides much stronger starting point for Diamond-level learning, improving exploration efficiency and reducing ineffective trial-and-error in the initial phase. Both Mixed Sampling (1:1) and Curriculum PretrainFreeze maintain steady upward trends and consistently dominate the two non-transfer baselines, indicating that lower-tier experience can be effectively reused for high-difficulty tasks. Diamond-only Self-learning also improves steadily, showing that selflearning on Diamond alone is feasible; however, its performance remains below both transfer-based strategies at every checkpoint. Meanwhile, Cold Start shows almost no meaningful improvement throughout training, which indicates that without any accumulated cross-episode knowledge, the agent struggles to make progress on Diamond tasks. Overall, these results support two conclusions. First, lower-tier knowledge transfers effectively to Diamond tasks and substantially improves both early and final performance. Second, among the two transfer-based strategies, Mixed Sampling (1:1) yields the strongest empirical results, suggesting that continuously updating unified knowledge base with both lower-tier and Diamond experiences is more effective than relying on frozen pretrained knowledge base alone. We present two qualitative case studies to illustrate how the proposed framework improves learning and decision making through knowledge distillation and failure-aware replanning. Figure 7 Case study of cross-episode learning from failure and success. failed crafting attempt is distilled into guardrail, which triggers replanning toward crafting table first. After success, the completed procedure is further distilled into reusable skill knowledge. Figure 6 illustrates how the failure taxonomy supports effective replanning. The agent first tries to approach target log, but repeated local motion near the terrain boundary indicates navigation oscillation pattern. This behavior is classified as NAV_OSCILLATE, which triggers re-evaluation of the current subgoal rather than continuing ineffective movement. After replanning, the agent identifies the blocking dirt structure as the immediate cause, changes the subgoal to breaking the dirt block first, and then proceeds to obtain the log. This case highlights that the proposed failure types are not merely descriptive labels; they provide grounded diagnostic signals that can be directly mapped to corrective subgoals, thereby improving robustness in long-horizon embodied tasks. 28 Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation Figure 7 shows representative example of cross-episode learning from both failure and success. In this case, the agent initially attempts to craft wooden pickaxe directly in the inventory interface and fails because the required recipe depends on crafting table. Instead of treating this attempt as discarded trajectory, the framework extracts reusable guardrail from the failure, namely that crafting table must be crafted before recipes requiring larger crafting grid can be executed. The agent then replans accordingly, crafts the crafting table, completes the correct recipe, and successfully obtains the target tool. Importantly, the successful trajectory is further distilled into reusable skill knowledge. This example shows that our framework does not learn only from successful executions; it also converts structured failures into actionable constraints, allowing the agent to avoid repeating the same mistake in future episodes while accumulating successful procedural knowledge.
